speaker: interviewer
text: --April 1995, and we're still in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Americans, they-- they gave us food and they made speeches on top of the tank. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. Always in Yiddish. And in English, whoever could speak English. Oh. And-- and then they told us at we [INAUDIBLE] louse, full with louse.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. It's OK. Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are in danger for the-- for the community. So they told us get as rid as much as possible from your clothes. We try to get you clothing, which they did. They came in a big truck with all khaki green uniforms and underwear and socks and-- and I don't know. But nothing fit. Can you imagine, the size of the smallest size that was that big.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how heavy were you at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: 37 Kilo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how heavy are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 85. [LAUGHTER] 85 kilo. I'm heavy now. I mean, a different-- never mind. And-- and they told us that we are in a little village there. And they had two hospitals there, that were two field hospitals. And they were fortunate full with SS, badly wounded SS. The names of the hotels were Post and Leader. I never forget those names. And we had to stay in that railway station. And they came with DDT. And I remember they say, [NON-ENGLISH]. We had to throw ourself and-- and try to get rid of many clothes as possible, if possible, undress yourself, if there was available. I got everything out and got the green-- I was all green and-- and white and dirty, you know, but the louse must have run away, left and right and everywhere. And it took about two hours. It was 6 o'clock in the-- it must have been 8, 9 o'clock-- I don't know times. In the meantime, we found out that the two hotels were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] full with SS. They disappeared in an hour, in tents. I've never seen the tents after. The trains pulled them away. They set up the two hotels, two hospitals, for us. And after two or three hours, we start marching through the hospitals. And well, in no time, the beds were full of people were dead. People were gone. I was still all right. I still walked. And they told me, yes, I'm sorry, you can't go here, because you're all right. Come back tomorrow. I come back tomorrow, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did-- where did you go overnight?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, day-- it was daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you say? Listen, any house, everywhere, any house, you go to any house you want to go. Just knock at the door and say, here I am. And you go in this accommodation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met another boy and we walked along there. And we knocked at a door. They were frightened. The white flag was out everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: But elderly people and-- inside and they gave us a room near a stable. There were-- I mean there were little animals there, you know, that were so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was inside. Inside, outside, and we were laying in the straw, you know, there was a little of it. It was a little-- it was not land. It was-- it was-- oh, how can I say it? [NON-ENGLISH]. It's a little farms, a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were laying there very comfortable for half an hour, and all of a sudden knocked at the door the MP. And-- and they looked around. The one said to me-- and I could speak some English-- what the bloody hell are you doing here? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took that gun, banging that door inside. And he told us [NON-ENGLISH]. That's all they could say, [NON-ENGLISH]. They have to get out of the house, and we had to get in the house. And we didn't. Then we-- we went in the-- in the main bedroom, and we lay down there. And then the MP, the Military Police, they left again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that was the first time you were in a bed for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 3 years nearly, 2, 2 and 1/2 years. And they had more rooms there, and they gave us a room. They were frightened for us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was frightened for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people. They were frightened for--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They had plenty to eat. And they made food for us, and we get to know them. And-- they were no-- they were no Nazis, you know. All the Nazis were gone. They were no-- no one was a Nazi anymore. [NON-ENGLISH] Hitler. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's what they said, they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that mean? What did they say in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that Hitler was bad, and we were-- you know, they all turned around like-- like a leaf. Never mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ach, no feeling for them. They were friendly. There were quite a few that pinched everything what they could get, watches and-- and-- they-- they stole everything. They took everything what they could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of us, you know. They saw something, they took it. There were no radios. But you had a watch or you had anything. I didn't. I couldn't. And we stayed there for two weeks. And we-- they had a little boat. We're sitting on the boat in the sun. And-- and we-- we recovered very nicely. And then the Americans, they got hold of me because I could speak English. And-- and they took over the-- their commandant too, their office, the German office. And they asked me, look, the prisoners came in by the thousands, the Germans. Germans came in by the thousands, you know, through. And many say, well, I-- I'm not a soldier. All the soldiers got rid of their uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and they say, [GERMAN] Holland, with an accent. And I had to listen to them, spoke to them. They say, is he Dutch? No, he's not. He's German. And they put them on the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, that net was so big, it gets smaller and smaller. Look, thousands escaped, you know. [INAUDIBLE] where did they go to? They went to Austria, back to Italy, to South America. They disappeared. Where were all those SS? What did it feel like to sit there and watch them come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, look, I tell you, the SS came. SS, [INAUDIBLE] the Wehrmacht came along, and they walked like that, some of them. And there was a little Polish boy. And he liked the belt from one of the Germans. And he asked the American. Ah, take it off. They let his pants fall off. I mean, it was-- it was unbelievable. But we were not cruel. Some of them were very cruel. They killed them. Some of them, they-- they killed the SS. They got their guns, or they got hold of something, and they shot them. I have never seen that, you know, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any desire? Did you have any strong feelings like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is very long ago. You see, the first years, yes. But there's a next generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. But at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. At that time, we hated them. We couldn't-- couldn't-- I had nothing to do with the Germans. We never spoke. I had only through the Americans. The Americans hated them, too. They were very rude, very cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember I was in Seeshaupt, and then I made good friends with the Americans. And one officer said to me, I'm going to Munich. Why don't you come with me, alone? And I went with him, in his Jeep. And before we came in Munich, there was a funny feeling. The Germans were standing in a line for milk. And he stopped that Jeep, and, you know, he had that flask-- how you call it?-- his flask. And he pushed in, and took milk, filled it up, and gave it to me. Things like that, you know, example. How did that feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, that feels like, like unbelievable. We were king. It went all so fast, you know, to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They knew who we were. And I stayed in Munich one night and was in transport. We want to go back home, home, home, home, back home. We want to go. You see, there were so many DPs, they couldn't go anywhere. And they want to get rid of the Dutch they had at home. You see, the-- the Polish people, they couldn't go home. They stayed in those camps. But they wanted to get rid of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a transport to Ulm. I'll be back in Ulm later. We went to Ulm. And in Ulm is a very big place there, a marketplace. And there were big tents there. And you could sleep there, you know, pick them up. We stayed there one night.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember there were black fellows who were driving those big trucks. And they were going fast. And we shouted, hey, listen. We got no bum yet. And-- and they gave us something to sit on. You know, we had no-- we still had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Ulm, I must tell you, we went to Mannheim. And in Mannheim, they got rid of me again the next day. I went to [INAUDIBLE]. There was a train, and we didn't know where the train went to, a good train. It was filled with French, mainly-- how you call it?-- prisoner of war, or-- not concentration camp, but they mixed them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had very nice, comfortable seating in the train, you know. It was not-- I wouldn't say it was first class, but good, what they could get. I remember in Saarlouis, in the Luxembourg, and priests were standing on the border with their-- and then-- and gave us a blessing. And then the train, it went up to Paris. And we arrived in Paris with thousands of people in the railway station, not waiting for us, waiting for their friends. And there I was speaked to by the Dutch authorities. There were not many Dutch in the [INAUDIBLE]. And we went to the Dutch consul. And in the Dutch consulate were two officers where we were registered. And I was in the first one. In the second one was my cousin sitting. I never met him. We found out the next-- a day later that I was there. We're close as cousins, still today. Still lives in Paris. And I stayed in Paris a few days. And we-- we had to eat food. We never paid. We went to the best restaurants and they-- they fed us. France was free. They could see we came out of camp, no hair, no nothing, no-- And then there was a train that went to Brussels. And we took the train to Brussels. And in Brussels, the Dutch survivors from there picked us up. They knew we were there. I don't know how. And they say, don't go to Holland. And Holland was completely empty. I stayed a month in Brussels. I had money in my pocket. I tell you, everyone put money in my pocket. I remember I-- we had clothes then. We started having a little bit clothes. That was a Joint-- you know, the Joint-- the American Jews, they gave clothing. And I had a beautiful coat. And there was one pocket was sewn through. And I took it apart, the-- the threads. There was $100 in it. I put it in-- $100 was a lot of money in those days. And then I-- I stayed in Brussels by people I met, and then we had invited and-- and then it was very hard to cross the border. They brought us to the border. And then I finished up in Amsterdam. There was no one there. My uncle, that's it. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to go back into Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible, terrible. No one there. My uncle very good, you know, my father's brother. I stayed with him. I was not allowed to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: By who?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle. He said, oh, you don't work. My uncle, he was quite-- my family was well off. And my uncle said, you don't work. Holiday. He wanted like it on holiday forever. So a few weeks, I did nothing. And then I went back. And I couldn't play. I couldn't play in the beginning. My hands were not there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? You couldn't physically, or the music wasn't there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had-- my hands were not-- I couldn't. And that was still--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you played since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I start playing after, not long, in Amsterdam and in Paris a while, a bit. And then I came here. I gave it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you give it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was too noisy. I don't know. I couldn't find-- no explanation for it. I came here. In '51, I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what were you doing when you-- before you-- in that six years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam, I-- I first [INAUDIBLE] diamond cutting, because I had some contacts there. And then my brother-in-law, who married the sister from my wife then, he started a factory in-- in domestic locks. And we started together. And he's still there, and he still makes locks. Very big. And we started to-- I did all the selling and he did all the manufacturing. I did that a few years. And my brother, he wanted to get out. And in 1949, he said he applied for license-- for a visa for America and for Australia. And he said the one that comes first, I go. So he finished up in Australia. And my sister-in-law came six months later. And then he wrote me, nice country here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had an uncle. He was working on the American embassy, American government, you know. He was a public servant. And I rang him, and I said, can you get me a visa? He said, yes, come next week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came, and I had a visa for three months here, three months. So I came here first class, [INAUDIBLE]. I had a terrific trip here. It was good. And I liked it here. And I start-- I came here on a Saturday, and I worked a Monday in Prouds, in the jeweler shop. I worked there for two years. And-- and then I-- I started doing a little bit business, and, you know, we saw some opportunities here. People got to sleep. And we were awaked and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you were awake?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We saw opportunities here. You know, they-- we started first in delicatessen shop. It's-- it was good in those days, because there was no where else where-- you couldn't get in Double Bay. I had a shop in Double Bay. And, must have been 1954, I met my wife. In those days, nice girl, Austrian. Look, she looked good in those days. And then after six months, we decided to go and to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to Queen's Square to fill in my papers. And I had a letter from the immigration department. I have to come. Who are you? I had no papers, because I was illegal here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, I'm here for three years. In those days, oh, you better come back next week. Yeah, OK. Stamp, stay, permanent. Not today anymore. Now they're saying get back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you didn't-- you didn't have any great desire to get out of Amsterdam? It just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It-- I was in Paris too for a while. I played even in Paris. I went to Paris. My cousin, my aunt was there, my uncle. And I played there for a little while. But no, I couldn't anymore. I was restless. I have to get out, different. You see, Amsterdam is Amsterdam. But it's not Amsterdam anymore. Today, it is a very pleasant city, but it's not the same. There's all memories and there's all-- I like to go back all the time, you know, but-- and then I come here and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Once. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can I tell that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, my neighbor asked me-- I forgot about this. My neighbor asked me if I could help him that he wanted to hide something next door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was '42. And yes, I went-- we had a garden and we digged a big hole in the ground. And there was a big box and we put it in the ground and covered it up. And that's it. I forgot about it. War was over. No-- out of my-- everything went out of my mind. A year later my aunt told me, your neighbor rang. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, next door. [? Ellerman. ?] I seen her and her daughter. Her husband died. They escaped to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she asked me, Sam, do you remember that you helped my husband digging a hole in the ground? I could have picked it up myself easy now. But I forgot about it. I wouldn't, I mean. I mean, you know, that's one of those things you don't know. I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the police. It was in 1945-- '46. It was a year later, '46. And we told them the story in the police. And two policemen came in a warrant in that house next door where I used to live. And it was an old lady living there, different people, very nice, no objections. You know, someone could be hostile and put that woman up-- don't worry about it. And the police, two-- two fellows to dig the hole in the ground, and a big case came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And what was it like to go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, terrible. It's-- I never been back since then. Matter of fact, we had sold my grandfather the factory in a part of Amsterdam. Fortunately, they demolished the whole street to make an expressway there. It's-- I don't mind the old parts where I went to go to school, the good memories. But where I've been deported from was-- was bad, you know. The-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you think that the experiences there has affected the way you've lived your life since?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: have lived very well after. I put in big screen for everything. And my wife was Austrian and never been through anything. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived my life. And I got one son, fortunate. He's very normal. He got no ill-- some people say the second generation-- he got no ill after-effects after the generations. He couldn't, no. I would never tell him. He was very sane and very straightforward and very clever. He got my brains, of course. [LAUGHTER] I don't know. And he also had two girls, beautiful girls. And that's it. I really feel that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just put a screen and forgot everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But now they tried me to bring it back and too much talk about-- about the museum and it is good. You see, I-- I'm really a survivor to say, you know, I-- I've been really through it. And I'm still glad that I can tell a little bit maybe for next generations. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to tell all the cruel things, I mean how people were hit and how we-- they were there. I've seen it. But you can't always tell it. There's is enough what I've told them to-- to keep it in memory, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's unbelievable how people went through. Unbelievable. And some people suffer still today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any-- is there something-- do you feel like your attitude changed at all as a result of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, you get older. I mean, when you're young, you have a different attitude as when you're very old, you know. You think different. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Am I-- how-- how do I know how I've been today, who I would be, don't know? I don't think so. Of course, your attitude is changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I got no photos sticking on the wall here, for one reason or the other one, because I-- I was looking for pictures of my father and my mother which I haven't got. You see, that's it. And then, now here. And then I say, well, I put my brother on the wall. And-- and that's it. See, that's my family there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll have a look at them in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, there's no one left. It's-- my sister-in-law was very more involved in it, because she's a different person, you see. Some people are very much involved. This is good. But not everyone can do it, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Fantastic.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall we-- let's meet the rest of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I call them? Are you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sam, could you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's my wife, [? Leah. ?] That's my daughter-in-law-- [? LEAH LISSING: ?] [? Judy. ?] Judy. That is [? Rebecca, ?] nearly five years. Near--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks' time, five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ooh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's [? Alexandra, ?] and she is seven years. And they're very, very sweet girls. They never fight. [LAUGHTER] Hmm? No, they're good girls. And we love them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything else you want to say to them all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] Yes. They can say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What would you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say? [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, come on. You can say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? JUDY LISSING: ?] Would you like to sing a Pesach song?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] No. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? LEAH LISSING: ?] No?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? JUDY LISSING: ?] Something you learned at school, [? Rebecca? ?] No. [? JUDY LISSING: ?] No. They're-- they're not shy. She is very good in-- in gymnastics. [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] We're having dinner at Oma's place. Yeah. She might go to the Olympics in-- [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] I'm not. --in the year 2000. [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] I'm not. Oh, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? LEAH LISSING: ?] Don't you want to sing a song? [? Alexandra? ?] [? Rebecca? ?] [? REBECCA LESSING: ?] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [? LEAH LISSING: ?] Because you will be on the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have a look there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? JUDY LISSING: ?] Do you want to sing together?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] No.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? LEAH LISSING: ?] It will be on the film. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you're old and gray, you can still look at it on the video. [? ALEXANDRA LISSING: ?] We're never going to be gray.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: There you go. All rightie. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sam, could you tell us who is in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Early in I think '41. She must have been 47 years. She was a healthy woman, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Angelena.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Dresden. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling and ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so this photo--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that photo is my uncles, two uncles, my father, my aunt, my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can we point them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nieces, my brother, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to lean forward--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: --and point them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's my mother and my father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my uncle, another uncle, another uncle, my grandmother, my brother-- they both survived the war. And that was my wife with my niece, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that was a-- a nephew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember names? Can you tell us names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ellia, ?] also [? Feita, ?] two [? Feitas ?] here, [? Marie, ?] Uncle Jack, [? Jacob. ?] My father's name was [? Meyer. ?] My grandmother was also [? Sophie. ?] My mother. That's [? Edith. ?] And that's [? Bep ?] and [? Herman. ?] They were-- they were still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my uncle at home. I think it was '42. It must be very close 'til the end-- could be '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last family photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say the last-- the photo we found back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was just a general family gathering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a-- just a gathering, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So who's in this photo, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my wife in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sophia. And she-- she perished, of course. We never seen her back. And that photo was in a shop in the window, you know. It was display. And someone saw that photo and told me. And I went there and they gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, maybe two years after the war. I didn't even know it was there, because we had nothing that-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that-- this was taken in about when?

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. I would say 1942. I mean, who takes-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a wedding photo or was it just a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Was no photo, wedding photos, nothing. It was just a photo taken. I don't know why. I can't even remember. One of those things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. So this is your number. And could you tell us what that number is? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how you were referred to, or by name?
